\documentclass{article}

\usepackage{amsmath}
\usepackage{amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}

\begin{document}

\title{A Toy Gronwall Bound}
\author{Test Author}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
We record an elementary Gronwall-type bound for a scalar differential
inequality. The proof is self-contained toy material.
\end{abstract}

\section{Setting}
Let $u : [0, T] \to \mathbb{R}$ be nonnegative and continuous, let $L > 0$, and
suppose the integral inequality
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:gronwall-hyp}
u(t) \le C_0 + L \int_0^t u(s) \, ds, \qquad 0 \le t \le T,
\end{equation}
holds for some constant $C_0 \ge 0$.

\begin{theorem}
\label{thm:gronwall}
Under the hypotheses above, $u(t) \le C_0 e^{L t}$ for all $t \in [0, T]$.
Consequently $u$ is bounded by a constant $C = C_0 e^{L T}$ that is
independent of the horizon $T$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Set $R(t) = C_0 + L \int_0^t u(s)\, ds$, so that $u(t) \le R(t)$ and
$R'(t) = L\, u(t) \le L\, R(t)$. Thus $\frac{d}{dt}\big(e^{-Lt} R(t)\big) \le 0$,
so $e^{-Lt} R(t) \le R(0) = C_0$, giving $R(t) \le C_0 e^{Lt}$ and hence
$u(t) \le C_0 e^{L t}$. Evaluating the exponential factor over the whole
interval yields the uniform bound $C = C_0 e^{L T}$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
